title: Executive program — Applied AI
blurb: Architecting AI systems in production, security and governance.
why: Wanted formal grounding in production AI architecture and governance to back up the hands-on work I'd been doing on SpeedFlow. Picked MIT's executive track over a longer commitment because it matched the depth-without-the-detour I needed.
highlights: Security and threat modeling for LLM applications
Governance frameworks (NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001) in practice

title: ISO/IEC 42001 — training (attestation)
blurb: AI management systems training — transparency, risk management, guardrails.
why: Took the official AFNOR training to align my SpeedFlow ISO 42001 work with the canonical French interpretation of the standard. Important nuance: this is a training attestation, not a certifying-auditor credential — the certification path is a separate multi-year track.
highlights: AI management system requirements per ISO/IEC 42001:2023
Risk assessment, transparency obligations, lifecycle controls
Bridges to ISO 27001 / 27701 (the work I did at L'Oréal)

title: Specialized master's
blurb: Strategy & entrepreneurship. Thesis on Wikipedia governance, cited internationally.
why: Came back to HEC after INSA + a first company (RDV Ophtalmo) to get the strategic and financial language I'd been missing during fundraising. The thesis topic — Wikipedia governance — picked itself, given the 17-year involvement.
highlights: Master's thesis on Wikipedia's emergent governance model, cited in Band & Gerafi's study on Wikipedia's economic value
Continued international academic citations of the thesis in the years that followed

title: Engineer's degree
why: INSA's combined engineer + science track was the obvious fit: rigorous CS, systems orientation, and the option to carry it into industry directly without a master's detour.
highlights: Computer science: systems, networks, software engineering
First production projects (intern + side gigs) during the curriculum
Foundation that's held up across 15 years of stack rotations
